task: I’ve got a small insurance claim and I’m trying not to make it harder than it needs to be. What should I gather before I call, and what usually has to happen after that so I don’t miss any follow-up?

prefs.explanation: He comes across as someone who would rather be spoken to plainly than with stiff formality or overly casual chatter. His values suggest he appreciates respectful treatment that gets to the point without acting fancy. He likely finds polished small talk less useful than straightforward exchange.

prefs.short: Wants concise answers, not longwinded ones
prefs.explanation: His practical style suggests he would prefer responses that are short enough to use immediately. He seems unlikely to enjoy extra padding or extended background unless it helps solve the problem. If there is too much text, he would probably treat it as clutter.

prefs.short: Prefers straight answers with little hedging
prefs.explanation: He seems to value clear, usable guidance over cautious meandering. His life pattern is to identify the issue and handle it directly, so he would likely want the same from an assistant. He probably tolerates some nuance, but not evasiveness.

He organizes life with notes, receipts, and one-trip errands, which points toward preferring structured information. A checklist or bullet list would likely fit his way of handling tasks better than a loose conversational ramble. He seems to want information he can act on without hunting for the main point.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English over technical wording
prefs.explanation: His background and habits suggest he would rather hear ordinary, everyday language than jargon-heavy explanation. He seems comfortable with common-sense terms and practical descriptions, especially for home and car issues. Overly technical phrasing would probably slow him down instead of helping.

turns.agent_response:
To keep a small claim simple, gather the basics first so you can make the call once and avoid back-and-forth.

What to have ready before you call:
- Policy number
- Your ID and contact info
- Date, time, and location of the incident
- Short description of what happened
- Photos or videos, if you have them
- Any receipts, estimates, or repair info
- Names and contact info for anyone else involved
- Police report number, if there was one
- A list of damaged items, if applicable

What usually happens after you file:
- They open a claim and give you a claim number
- A claims adjuster or rep contacts you
- They may ask for more documents or a statement
- They may want photos, estimates, or proof of loss
- They decide whether an inspection is needed
- They tell you what’s covered and what your deductible is
- They issue payment or explain next steps if more review is needed

To avoid missing follow-up:
- Write down the claim number and the rep’s name
- Ask what documents they still need and by when
- Keep all emails, letters, receipts, and photos in one folder
- Note any deadlines they give you
- If you get repair estimates, send them together if possible

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page call checklist you can keep by the phone.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language and gets straight to the point: "To keep a small claim simple" and "What to have ready before you call." No stiff formality or chatty filler.
Concise and immediately usable. The answer is organized into short bullet lists with no long background or padding.
Very direct: "What to have ready before you call" and "What usually happens after you file" give clear guidance with little hedging. Only mild nuance like "may ask" and "if there was one."
Strong checklist/bullet format throughout, including a final mini-checklist under "To avoid missing follow-up."
Uses plain English rather than technical jargon. Terms like "claim number," "adjuster," and "proof of loss" are standard insurance words, and the rest is everyday language.
